title: What's new and updated for payroll - Canada.ca
content:
## What's new and updated for payroll
Find out about new and updated payroll content, including legislative changes and Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) administrative policies. You can also learn about payroll consultations or upcoming events, who can attend and how to register for free webinars hosted by the CRA.
- Open or manage a payroll account
- Set up and manage employee payroll information
- Calculate payroll deductions and contributions
- Remit (pay) payroll deductions and contributions
- File payroll information returns (slips and summaries)

## Other payroll topics
- Compliance and enforcement
- New content - Shareholder benefits Reason for new content: Clarity, completeness, and plain language Effective date: Not applicable Refer to: Shareholder benefits Brief description You (as a corporation) may provide benefits to one of your shareholders in their capacity as a shareholder or to a related person or an affiliated person of your shareholder. Shareholder benefits can take almost any form. However, shareholder benefits do not include benefits a shareholder receives in their capacity as an employee . Published on: June 1, 2026

- New content - Reassessments after payroll compliance reviews Reason for new content: Clarity, completeness, and plain language Effective date: Not applicable Refer to: Reassessments after payroll compliance reviews Brief description Following a payroll compliance review, a payroll trust accounts examination, or an employer compliance audit, T4 or T4A slips may be corrected to reflect accurate income, withholdings, or other tax-related information of workers. - Rewritten content - Payments to emergency services volunteers Reason for change: Clarity, completeness, and plain language Effective date: Not applicable Refer to: Payments to emergency services volunteers Brief description You (as a government, municipality, or public authority) may pay an individual who performs the duties of certain emergency services workers on a volunteer basis (an emergency service volunteer). An individual cannot be considered a volunteer if, in the same year, they were employed by the same public authority to perform the same or similar duties as the volunteer activities. Depending on the situation, the individual may qualify for an exemption of up to $1,000 from the amounts you paid them, provided all applicable conditions are met. Content has been updated for clarity, completeness, and plain language. No changes were made to the current CRA administrative policy. Published on: April 29, 2026

- Rewritten content - Housing or utilities - Provided to a member of the clergy, a regular minister or a member of a religious order Reason for change: Clarity, completeness, and plain language Effective date: Not applicable Refer to: Housing or utilities - Provided to a member of the clergy, a regular minister or a member of a religious order Brief description Generally, if you provide free or low-rent accommodation or utilities or an allowance  for housing or utilities to your employee, the benefit or allowance is taxable. This includes situations where you provide your employee with housing rent-free or for less than the fair market value (FMV) of the accommodation. Depending on the situation, your employee who is a member of the clergy may be able to claim a deduction from their employment income for their residence when filing a personal income tax return. If they choose to claim this deduction, you may have to reduce the income used to calculate CPP and income tax withholdings by the amount of the deduction. - New CRA administrative policy Payments for trucking services Reason for change: Lifting of the moratorium on penalties for businesses operating in the trucking industry for failing to report fees for services paid to a Canadian controlled private corporation in the trucking industry using box 048 of the T4A slip for certain corporations who earn income primary from trucking Effective date: Slips filed for 2025 and later calendar years Refer to: Payments for trucking services and T4A slip - Information for payers and CRA strengthens compliance in trucking sector by lifting the moratorium on T4A penalties Brief description New CRA administrative policy For 2025 and later calendar years , businesses operating in the trucking industry will be assessed penalties for failing to report fees for services exceeding $500 in a calendar year that they pay to a Canadian-controlled private corporation in the trucking industry using box 048 of the T4A slip. Published on: January 5, 2025

- Reason for change: Lifting of the moratorium on penalties for businesses operating in the trucking industry for failing to report fees for services paid to a Canadian controlled private corporation in the trucking industry using box 048 of the T4A slip for certain corporations who earn income primary from trucking
- Effective date: Slips filed for 2025 and later calendar years
- Refer to: Payments for trucking services and T4A slip - Information for payers and CRA strengthens compliance in trucking sector by lifting the moratorium on T4A penalties

Published on: December 2, 2025
New CRA administrative policy Reporting of security options benefits and related deductions
- Reason for change: Change to T4 reporting for security options benefits and related deductions
- Effective date: Slips filed for calendar year 2025 and after
- Refer to: Employee security (stock) options and T4 slip – Information for employers
Under the new CRA administrative policy, for the reporting of security options benefits and related deductions received in the 2025 calendar year, you can use either:
- Codes 38, 39 and 41, for slips filed on or after January 12, 2026
For the reporting of security options benefits and related deductions received in the 2026 calendar year and after, you will only use codes 38, 39 and 41.

## Under proposed legislation CRA administrative policy for reporting Employee security (stock) options
- Reason for change: Postponement of legislative change
- Effective date: Benefits provided from June 25, 2024 to December 31, 2024
- Refer to: Employee security (stock) options and T4 slip – Information for employers
New CRA administrative policy, for the reporting of security options benefits and related deductions which occurred during the period from June 25, 2024 to December 31, 2024 , if:
- You have not updated your payroll system to use the new codes 90, 91 and 92: You can use code 38 to report the benefit, and report the deduction as 1/2 of the value reported in code 38 using either code 39 or 41
- You have updated your payroll system to use the new codes 90, 91 and 92: You can use the new code 90 to report the benefit, and report the deduction as 1/3 of the value reported in code 90 using either code 91 or 92 You can use the code 38 to report the benefit, and report the deduction as 1/2 of the value reported in code 38 using either code 39 or 41
- You can use the new code 90 to report the benefit, and report the deduction as 1/3 of the value reported in code 90 using either code 91 or 92
- You can use the code 38 to report the benefit, and report the deduction as 1/2 of the value reported in code 38 using either code 39 or 41
This policy was introduced to reduce the burden on employers and payroll service providers as a result of the proposed changes which were to take effect June 25, 2024, but that will now become effective on January 1, 2026.
